core_intuitions: Decoupling the SOURCE of supervision (cheap human comparisons) from its FORM (a dense, differentiable scalar reward defined over ALL responses) is why RLHF can exceed SFT: the policy generates and grades its own training data, exploring outputs better than any demonstration, and learns from negative contrast, not just imitation.

Bradley-Terry converts pairwise comparisons into a latent scalar strength: P(y_w > y_l) = sigma(r_w - r_l), so the reward IS a log-odds of preference and only score DIFFERENCES are identified (adding a constant to every score changes nothing).

The RM loss -log sigma(Delta) = softplus(-Delta) is self-limiting: near-zero loss and push when the model is confidently correct (large +Delta), log 2 at Delta=0, and a saturating push of magnitude (1 - sigma(Delta)) when confidently wrong.

The reward model is a Siamese shared-encoder comparator: the SFT network with the LM head replaced by a scalar head read at the last token, run on chosen and rejected through the SAME weights, with only their difference entering the loss; all C(K,2) pairs from one prompt are encoded once (efficiency + anti-overfit).

The RM is a proxy and the policy is its adversary (Goodhart): optimizing the proxy over-optimizes it, so true quality rises then FALLS as KL from the reference grows, making the KL leash and early-stopping on a held-out gold signal non-negotiable.

The PPO stage juggles four models (actor and critic trained, reward and reference frozen): each token pays KL 'rent' to the frozen reference and the sequence collects the RM's scalar at the last token; reference controls distribution drift, critic controls gradient variance.

new_figures.name: bradley-terry-loss-shape
new_figures.concept: How the Bradley-Terry pairwise loss maps the RM's score gap Delta = r_w - r_l to a preference probability sigma(Delta) and a self-limiting loss -log sigma(Delta), and why only differences matter.
new_figures.rationale: The Bradley-Terry model is the mathematical heart of the chapter ('the bridge from discrete comparisons to a continuous reward') and currently has NO figure. Three of the chapter's most-repeated intuitions are inherently visual and only appear as prose/equations: the sigmoid mapping from score-gap to preference probability (score = log-odds), the softplus loss shape with its three regimes, and the self-limiting gradient (push proportional to how wrong the model is). Seeing the curves makes 'the loss and gradient scale with how wrong the model is' and 'only gaps are meaningful' click instantly.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The Bradley-Terry model: from comparisons to a scalar', immediately after the '### Deriving the pairwise loss' subsection (after the gradient equation dL/dDelta = -(1 - sigma(Delta))).
new_figures.brief: A single static infographic built around one horizontal axis: the margin Delta = r_w - r_l (the score gap the RM assigns the chosen minus rejected response). Two stacked, x-aligned curves share this axis. TOP curve: the preference probability sigma(Delta), an S-curve rising from 0 to 1; mark and label three points -- sigma(0) = 0.5 ('coin flip: equal scores'), sigma(+2) approx 0.88 ('chosen preferred ~88% of the time'), and note in a caption 'the score IS a log-odds of preference'. BOTTOM curve (x-aligned beneath): the per-example loss L(Delta) = -log sigma(Delta) = softplus(-Delta), a smooth decreasing curve. Annotate its THREE regimes with callouts: far right (large +Delta) 'confidently correct: loss -> 0, gradient -> 0 (almost no push)'; center at Delta=0 'L = log 2 approx 0.69'; far left (large -Delta) 'confidently WRONG: loss grows ~linearly, gradient saturates at magnitude 1 (strong push to flip)'. On the loss curve, draw two small tangent/arrow glyphs of DIFFERENT lengths -- a tiny arrow on the right (small push) and a long arrow on the left (big push) -- labeled 'push proportional to (1 - sigma(Delta)): self-limiting'. Include a small self-contained INSET panel titled 'Only differences matter': a mini number line with two dots r_w and r_l and a bracket showing Delta between them; below it, the same two dots shifted right by a constant c, with the bracket showing the SAME Delta; caption 'shift both scores by any c -> Delta, sigma(Delta), loss, and gradient all unchanged. Reward is identified only up to an additive constant.' Use small illustrative integers only (Delta values 0, +2, -2; treat r_w, r_l as unlabeled positions or tiny integers). Theme-safe: one accent color for the sigma curve, a second muted accent for the loss curve; no hardcoded page-background-dependent fills; everything inside the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading order (axis label, sigma curve, loss curve, inset). Invent NO benchmark numbers; sigma(2) approx 0.88 and log 2 approx 0.69 are exact math, fine to state.

new_figures.name: rm-siamese-comparator
new_figures.concept: The reward model trains as a Siamese (weight-tied) shared-encoder comparator: chosen and rejected pass through the SAME network, only their scalar difference enters the loss, and all C(K,2) pairs from one prompt are formed from K encodings done once.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter explicitly and repeatedly stresses that the RM is a 'Siamese / shared-encoder' comparator where 'both responses pass through the SAME network and only the difference of its two outputs ever enters the loss' -- and that the InstructGPT anti-overfitting/efficiency trick is to encode all K completions of one prompt ONCE and form all C(K,2) pairwise terms cheaply. The existing rm-scalar-head figure shows a single forward pass producing one scalar; it does NOT show the pairwise comparator TRAINING structure or the weight-tying, which is interview-critical ('the central code of the chapter') and currently unvisualized. This figure completes the architecture story: head (existing) + comparator (new).
new_figures.placement: Section 'Reward model architecture and training', at the end of the '### The from-scratch pairwise loss' subsection (right after the paragraph noting the shared-encoder/Siamese structure and that only the difference enters the loss).
new_figures.brief: A two-panel static figure. LEFT PANEL ('the pairwise comparator'): two parallel horizontal tracks. Top track = prompt x ++ chosen response y_w; bottom track = prompt x ++ rejected response y_l. Each track flows into a box labeled 'Reward model r_phi (scalar head at last token)'. Draw the two boxes as visibly the SAME network -- link them with a lock/equals glyph and a label 'WEIGHTS TIED (one network, two forward passes)'. Each box emits a scalar: r_w (top) and r_l (bottom). The two scalars converge into a small subtractor node computing Delta = r_w - r_l, feeding a box '-log sigma(Delta)  (Bradley-Terry loss)'. Emphasize with a highlighted label 'only the DIFFERENCE enters the loss'. Add dashed gradient-flow arrows running BACKWARD from the loss through BOTH copies to the shared weights, labeled 'both forward passes update the same weights'. RIGHT PANEL ('all K pairs from one prompt, encoded once'): one prompt x at top, fanning out to K completions (use K=4: y_1..y_4, note 'ranked by the human, y_1 best'). Each completion is encoded ONCE by the same shared r_phi (draw four small tied encoder glyphs or one encoder with four passes) -> four scalars r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4. From those four scalars draw a compact triangular grid of the C(4,2)=6 pairwise differences (r_i - r_j for i<j), labeled 'C(K,2)=6 comparisons = cheap arithmetic on K=4 scalars'. Caption the panel: 'Encode K completions once, form all C(K,2) pairs in ONE forward/backward pass -- InstructGPT's efficiency win AND anti-overfitting regularizer.' Use small illustrative integers only (K=4, 6 pairs). Theme-safe: use surface/accent tokens, no hardcoded white or primary fills; ensure both panels fit the viewBox with a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading order and no overlapping labels. Invent no benchmark numbers.

new_figures.name: why-rlhf-beats-sft
new_figures.concept: The key conceptual move of RLHF: decouple the SOURCE of supervision (cheap human comparisons) from its FORM (a dense scalar reward over all responses), letting the policy generate and grade its own data and exceed the SFT demonstration ceiling.
new_figures.rationale: This is the paragraph the chapter itself calls 'The key conceptual move', and it is the single reason RLHF > SFT -- yet it is only prose. It also replaces the redundant second pipeline figure at exactly the right marker (immediately before the decoupling paragraph), turning a duplicate into a load-bearing intuition figure. It visualizes what no other figure does: that SFT can only imitate a fixed set of demonstrations (a ceiling), whereas the RM generalizes preferences into a scalar surface over the WHOLE response space, and the policy then explores beyond the demonstrations toward the high-reward region, learning from negative contrast too.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The three-stage recipe and why it works', at the marker currently occupied by {{fig:rlhf-three-stage-pipeline}} (line 15), immediately before the 'The key conceptual move is decoupling...' paragraph. This REPLACES that redundant figure.
new_figures.brief: A static comparison infographic contrasting SFT's ceiling with RLHF's reach, organized as a left-to-right story. LEFT ('SFT: imitate demonstrations'): a small cluster of a FIXED, finite set of dots labeled 'human-written demonstrations' sitting on a shaded band labeled 'the demonstration ceiling -- every token weighted equally, no notion of better-vs-best'. Show the SFT policy pinned to this cluster. CENTER ('decouple source from form'): two side-by-side chips -- SOURCE chip: 'human COMPARISONS (y_w > y_l): cheap, low-variance, no expertise, but sparse and discrete'; FORM chip: 'reward model r_phi(x,y): a DENSE, differentiable scalar defined over the ENTIRE response space, including responses no human has ever seen'. An arrow from SOURCE to FORM labeled 'RM generalizes preferences into a scalar surface'. Render the reward as a smooth shaded landscape/heat surface over an abstract 'space of all responses' plane, with the demonstration cluster shown as just a small patch on it. RIGHT ('RLHF: policy explores beyond the demos'): show the policy proposing NEW responses (dots outside the demonstration cluster), the RM grading each with r_phi, and the policy being nudged toward the high-reward region -- an arrow labeled 'policy generates its own training data, climbs toward high reward, and learns from the CONTRAST (what NOT to do), not just imitation'. Mark clearly that the reached high-reward region lies OUTSIDE/ABOVE the demonstration ceiling ('outputs better than any demonstration in the dataset'). Keep it a single coherent left-to-right narrative with three captioned zones. Theme-safe: use accent tokens for the reward surface (a light illustrative gradient, not a hardcoded page color), surface tokens for chips; all labels legible in both themes, nothing clipped, clear reading order. Small illustrative counts only (a handful of dots); invent no numbers.

summary: The chapter is figure-rich but has one redundancy and one glaring gap. Redundancy: rlhf-pipeline and rlhf-three-stage-pipeline are near-duplicate three-stage overviews placed on consecutive lines; keep the cleaner, theme-safe rlhf-pipeline and REPLACE the three-stage duplicate (which also hardcodes primary-purple headers and non-adapting white boxes) with a figure serving the chapter's self-declared 'key conceptual move' -- why RLHF beats SFT via decoupling source-from-form of supervision (new: why-rlhf-beats-sft, at the same marker). Gap: the Bradley-Terry model, called the mathematical bridge and heart of the chapter, has NO figure; propose bradley-terry-loss-shape (the sigma(Delta) preference curve, the softplus loss with its three regimes and self-limiting gradient, and the 'only differences matter' invariance). The Siamese shared-encoder COMPARATOR training structure (weight-tied two-forward-pass, difference-only loss, all-C(K,2)-pairs-encoded-once) is stressed in prose and interview-critical but unvisualized -- propose rm-siamese-comparator to complete the architecture story alongside the existing scalar-head figure. The remaining existing figures are keepers with concrete dark-mode fixes: rm-scalar-head (Linear/backbone boxes use non-adapting white fills, illegible in dark; h_{T-1} label overlap), rm-overoptimization-curve (invisible dark-mode zone shading, a couple labels on the curves), and rlhf-four-model-setup (panels 3-4 hardcoded white in dark, low-contrast caption row). Net: 3 new figures (2 priority-1 filling real conceptual gaps, 1 priority-2 replacing a duplicate), 1 keep, 3 refine, 1 replace.
